method_atom: An evolution equation for a time-varying probability distribution is recast as the steepest-descent (gradient) flow of a relative-entropy / free-energy functional over the metric space of probability measures endowed with an optimal-transport (Wasserstein) distance. When the driving functional is convex (its reference measure log-concave), this yields general existence of the dynamics together with a priori estimates on the evolving distributions, and a stability/continuity principle: weak convergence of the equilibrium (minimizing) measures forces convergence in law of the associated stochastic dynamics.
object_atom: Markov diffusion processes generated by stochastic differential equations whose drift is the gradient of a convex functional and whose invariant measures are log-concave; concretely instantiated in stochastic partial differential equations and in the equilibrium fluctuations of a class of random interfaces.
brief_reason: The entropy-gradient-flow-in-Wasserstein-metric machinery plus its convexity-driven existence and weak-convergence stability is the transferable method; the specific gradient-drift SDEs, their log-concave measures, and the random-interface/SPDE systems are the domain-specific object, with the two cleanly separable.
